learning_objectives: Define project risk and distinguish threats from opportunities
Describe the risk management process from planning through monitoring
Develop a risk management plan for a project
readings: Hillson, Practical Project Risk Management, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Every project faces uncertainty. Risk management is the systematic process of identifying, analyzing, and responding to project risks.
key_concepts: Risk definition: uncertain event or condition that affects objectives positively or negatively
Threats (negative risks) vs. opportunities (positive risks)
Risk management plan: methodology, roles, categories, probability/impact definitions, reporting
Risk appetite, tolerance, and threshold
Risk breakdown structure (RBS)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing a risk management plan from a real infrastructure project and identifying its components

review.value: A risk is a future uncertain event; an issue is a risk that has materialized and requires immediate action
The degree of uncertainty an organization is willing to accept; influences how aggressively risks are managed

learning_objectives: Apply brainstorming, Delphi, and SWOT analysis for risk identification
Create a risk register with proper risk statements
Use checklists and historical data to identify common risks
readings: PMI Practice Standard for Risk Management, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot manage risks you have not identified. A structured approach ensures comprehensive risk identification.
key_concepts: Brainstorming: facilitated sessions with project team and stakeholders
Delphi technique: anonymous expert input with iterative convergence
SWOT analysis: strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats
Risk statement format: cause-risk-effect (Because of X, Y may occur, leading to Z)
Risk categories: technical, external, organizational, project management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Facilitating a risk identification brainstorming session for a new product launch project

review.value: Cause-Risk-Effect format ensures clarity: Because of [cause], [risk event] may occur, leading to [impact on objectives]
Anonymous expert input prevents groupthink and anchoring bias; iterate until consensus emerges

learning_objectives: Assess risk probability and impact using defined scales
Construct and use a probability-impact matrix
Prioritize risks for further analysis or response planning

lecture_notes.introduction: Qualitative risk analysis prioritizes risks by assessing their probability of occurrence and impact on objectives.
key_concepts: Probability scales: very low (0.1), low (0.3), moderate (0.5), high (0.7), very high (0.9)
Impact scales: defined for each objective (scope, time, cost, quality)
Probability-Impact (P-I) matrix: risk score = probability x impact
Risk urgency: how soon the risk could occur
Risk categorization: grouping by RBS category for pattern analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rating 10 identified risks using a P-I matrix and ranking them by risk score
setup_requirements: Risk register with identified risks from previous session

review.value: Multiplying probability by impact produces a risk score; higher scores get priority attention
Qualitative analysis is subjective; use consistent scales and multiple assessors to reduce bias

learning_objectives: Perform Expected Monetary Value (EMV) analysis using decision trees
Understand Monte Carlo simulation concepts for schedule and cost risk
readings: Vose, Risk Analysis: A Quantitative Guide, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Quantitative risk analysis numerically estimates the combined effect of identified risks on project objectives.
key_concepts: Expected Monetary Value: EMV = probability x monetary impact for each risk
Decision tree analysis: modeling decisions and chance events with branches
Monte Carlo simulation: running thousands of scenarios to produce probability distributions
Sensitivity analysis: identifying which risks have the greatest impact on objectives
Tornado diagram: ranks risks by their impact on a specific objective
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a decision tree for a make-vs-buy decision with three risk scenarios
setup_requirements: Monte Carlo simulation software (Crystal Ball or @RISK trial)

review.value: Sum of EMV for all risks gives the project contingency reserve amount needed
Produces an S-curve of possible outcomes; the 80th percentile value is often used for contingency

learning_objectives: Select appropriate response strategies for threats and opportunities
Develop risk response plans with owners, triggers, and actions

lecture_notes.introduction: After analysis, each risk needs a planned response. The right strategy depends on the risk's priority, the organization's risk tolerance, and cost-effectiveness.
key_concepts: Threat responses: avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept (active and passive)
Opportunity responses: exploit, share, enhance, accept
Escalate: for risks outside the project manager's authority
Risk response plan: owner, trigger, action, timeline, cost
Residual risk: remaining risk after response; secondary risk: new risk created by the response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing response plans for the top 5 risks from the qualitative analysis exercise

review.value: Avoid eliminates the risk entirely (change plan); mitigate reduces probability or impact to an acceptable level
Shifts the financial impact to a third party (insurance, contracts); does not eliminate the risk, only the financial burden

class_schedule.topic: Risk Monitoring, Control, and Organizational Risk Maturity
learning_objectives: Implement risk monitoring and control processes
Assess organizational risk maturity and build a risk culture

lecture_notes.introduction: Risk management is continuous. Risks must be monitored throughout the project, and the risk register is a living document.
key_concepts: Risk reassessment: periodically re-evaluating identified risks and identifying new ones
Risk audits: examining the effectiveness of risk responses
Risk status reporting: including risk updates in project status reports
Lessons learned: capturing risk management successes and failures for future projects
Organizational risk maturity: ad hoc, repeatable, defined, managed, optimizing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a risk audit review on a case study project mid-execution

review.value: Evaluates whether risk responses were effective, risk owners performed their duties, and the risk process was followed
Higher maturity organizations have consistent risk processes, trained staff, and risk-aware culture across all projects

questions.question: Write a properly formatted risk statement (cause-risk-effect) for a software development project where the lead developer might leave mid-project.
questions.answer: Because of a competitive job market and lack of retention incentives (cause), the lead developer may resign during the development phase (risk event), leading to a 4-6 week schedule delay while a replacement is recruited and onboarded, plus potential rework due to knowledge loss (effect on objectives).

questions.question: A risk has a probability of 0.3 and an impact score of 0.7 on a 0-1 scale. Another risk has a probability of 0.7 and an impact of 0.3. Both have the same risk score. Explain why qualitative analysis alone may not be sufficient to differentiate them.
questions.answer: Both risks have a P-I score of 0.21, but they present different challenges. The first risk (low probability, high impact) is a low-frequency catastrophic event requiring contingency planning. The second risk (high probability, low impact) is likely to occur but with manageable consequences, better addressed through mitigation. Qualitative analysis treats them equally, but quantitative analysis (EMV with dollar values) would differentiate them based on actual monetary exposure.

rubric.key: Recognizes both have equal P-I scores
Explains different risk profiles despite equal scores

questions.question: A project faces two risks: Risk A has a 40% probability of causing a $50,000 loss. Risk B has a 25% probability of causing an $80,000 loss. Calculate the EMV for each risk and the total contingency reserve needed.
For Risk A above, explain why 'transfer' might be a better response strategy than 'mitigate,' and provide one specific transfer mechanism.
questions.answer: Transfer may be better than mitigate because the risk has a moderate probability (40%) and significant financial impact ($50,000). If the cost of mitigation activities approaches or exceeds the EMV ($20,000), transfer becomes more cost-effective. A specific transfer mechanism would be purchasing an insurance policy or including a penalty clause in the vendor contract that shifts the financial burden for the risk event to the vendor.

rubric.key: Compares cost-effectiveness of transfer vs. mitigate

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Risk Management Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete risk management plan for a project including risk identification (minimum 15 risks), qualitative analysis, quantitative analysis for top risks, and response plans.
objectives: Identify at least 15 risks using structured techniques
Perform qualitative analysis with a P-I matrix
Conduct EMV analysis for the top 5 risks
Develop response plans with owners and triggers for all high-priority risks
deliverables: Risk register with 15+ risks, qualitative ratings, and response strategies
EMV analysis and decision tree for top 5 risks
Risk response plan for all high and very high risks

criteria: At least 15 risks identified across multiple categories
Risks cover technical, external, organizational, and PM categories
Consistent probability and impact scales applied
Risk prioritization is logical and well-reasoned
EMV correctly calculated for top 5 risks
Decision tree properly constructed with probabilities and payoffs
Appropriate strategy selected for each risk
Response plans include owner, trigger, action, and timeline

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on risk identification, analysis, and response techniques

midterm_examination.description: Risk identification, qualitative analysis, and P-I matrix construction

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam with emphasis on quantitative analysis and response planning

class_participation.description: Participation in risk workshops, case studies, and group risk identification exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Risk identification workshops require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Risk identification workshops are collaborative. The risk management plan assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all sources. Risk data fabrication is treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet application required. Monte Carlo simulation software (trial versions acceptable) recommended for quantitative analysis.
